setting.note: When Sir Charles Baskerville dies of fright near a legend of a spectral hound, Sherlock Holmes sends Dr Watson to guard the heir on the lonely wastes of Dartmoor while he works unseen. Doyle's most famous case maps a real and forbidding landscape: the Baker Street opening, Baskerville Hall on the moor, the treacherous Grimpen Mire, and the fog-bound heath across which the great hound comes.

rights.originalWork: Public domain. Arthur Conan Doyle (d. 1930), first published 1902. Mapped as a cited work: we store no text and reproduce no passages here. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. This entry reproduces no text; the public-domain original is widely available.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. The original text is in the public domain.
rights.attribution: The Hound of the Baskervilles, Arthur Conan Doyle (1902). Locations mapped by Read the Places.

palette.note: The grey of the Dartmoor fog and stone out of which the phosphorescent hound comes bounding, the murk that hides a very human crime behind a legend. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
